---
title: "LLM Hardware Specification"
---

Let me break down the key factors/matrices to consider when sizing hardware for running an LLM.

## High-Level View

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         LLM Hardware Requirements Evaluation            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐   ┌─────────────┐  │
│  │    MODEL     │ → │   WORKLOAD   │ → │   SYSTEM    │  │
│  │   (what?)    │   │    (how?)    │   │  (where?)   │  │
│  └──────────────┘   └──────────────┘   └─────────────┘  │
│                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 1. Model Factors (what you're running)

| Factor | Examples | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Parameters (N)** | 7B, 13B, 70B, 405B | Dominant memory cost |
| **Precision (P)** | FP32, FP16/BF16, INT8, INT4 | Bytes per parameter |
| **Architecture** | Dense vs. MoE (e.g., Mixtral) | MoE activates subset → less compute, same VRAM |
| **Context length** | 4K, 32K, 128K, 1M tokens | Drives KV-cache size |
| **Tokenizer / vocab** | 32K–250K | Small effect on embedding size |

**Precision cheat sheet (bytes per param):**

```
FP32   ████████  4.0 bytes
FP16   ████      2.0 bytes   ← common default
BF16   ████      2.0 bytes
INT8   ██        1.0 byte
INT4   █         0.5 bytes   ← popular for laptops
```

## 2. Workload Factors (how you're running it)

| Factor | Question to ask |
|---|---|
| **Task type** | Inference, fine-tuning, or training from scratch? |
| **Batch size** | 1 user at a time, or N concurrent? |
| **Latency target** | Tokens/sec needed? (chat ≈ 20 t/s, batch jobs lower OK) |
| **Throughput** | Requests/sec for serving? |
| **Prompt length** | Long-context (RAG, docs) or short chat? |

Training/fine-tuning is **3–4× more memory-hungry** than inference because of optimizer states and gradients.

## 3. System Factors (where you're running it)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MEMORY           ← usually the bottleneck
│  ├── VRAM (NVIDIA/AMD GPU)             
│  └── Unified memory (Apple Silicon)    
│                                         
│  COMPUTE                                
│  ├── GPU: tensor cores, CUDA cores     
│  ├── CPU: cores, AVX/AVX-512           
│  └── NPU: Apple Neural Engine, NPUs    
│                                         
│  MEMORY BANDWIDTH ← often the real      
│  (GB/s)             bottleneck for       
│                     inference           
│                                         
│  STORAGE                                
│  └── NVMe SSD for model load speed     
│                                         
│  INTERCONNECT                           
│  └── NVLink/PCIe for multi-GPU         
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## The Core Memory Equation (for inference)

```
Total VRAM ≈ Model Weights + KV Cache + Activations + Overhead

Model Weights  = N_params × bytes_per_param
KV Cache       ≈ 2 × n_layers × n_heads × head_dim × seq_len
                 × batch × bytes_per_param
Overhead       ≈ 1–2 GB (framework, CUDA kernels)
```

**Quick rule of thumb for weights only:**

| Model | FP16 | INT8 | INT4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7B | ~14 GB | ~7 GB | ~3.5 GB |
| 13B | ~26 GB | ~13 GB | ~6.5 GB |
| 70B | ~140 GB | ~70 GB | ~35 GB |

Then add **20–30%** for KV cache + overhead.

## Why Memory Bandwidth ≠ Memory Capacity

For inference, the GPU/CPU must stream every weight for each token generated. So:

```
Max tokens/sec ≈ Memory Bandwidth (GB/s) / Model Size (GB)
```
